complete_examples.overview:
Worked examples demonstrating the redesigned vision discovery process with interactive mock generation, state.json progress tracking, and multi-spawn resume. These show the interview rhythm — how to question, when to generate mocks, how to handle CSS fixation, and how resume works across spawns.
File paths shown are relative to the vision/ output directory.

example.name: task_management_app_full_discovery
example.scenario: An orchestrator spawns a Vision Hasher subtask: "Hash out the vision for a task management app. The user wants something like Trello but with built-in time tracking. Templates are at agent-workspace/project-director/vision/."

step.approach: VH checks: vision/state.json does not exist → fresh start. Reads templates: app-overview.md, personas.md, feature-inventory.md, data/ directory (empty — entities discovered during interview), screens/ directory (empty — screens discovered during interview), flows/ directory (empty), edge-cases.md. Parses brief: "like Trello but with time tracking." Creates state.json with current_phase="foundation", spawn_count=1. Presents plan: "5 phases — Foundation, Screens (with interactive mocks), Flows, Edge Cases, Wire-Up. Ready?" User confirms → Phase 1 begins.

VH interviews through app-overview → personas → feature-inventory. On feature-inventory: user describes 28 features across Core, Secondary, and Nice-to-Have tiers. Each feature gets a paragraph description in feature-inventory.md. Feature names are added to state.json planned.features.
VH: "For each core feature, I'll create a detailed spec file in features/. Let's drill into Time Tracking first..."
Uses feature_specification question pattern. Fills features/time-tracking.md with: goal, trigger, step-by-step behavior, data in/out, validation, success/failure states, permissions, interactions with other features.
Repeats for all core features. Secondary and Nice-to-Have features get inventory entries only (detailed specs deferred — marked in state.json).
Data entities discovered during feature interviews: User, Project, Task, TimeEntry, Report, Notification, Team. VH creates data/*.md for each using data_concept question pattern.
step.filled_section_example:
features/time-tracking.md excerpt: --- The Time Tracking feature MUST allow users to record time spent on tasks.
Trigger: User clicks the ▶️ Play button on a task card (screens/task-card.html) or opens the task detail view and clicks "Start Timer."
Happy Path: 1. User clicks ▶️ Play → timer starts counting up on the task card. 2. Play button transforms to ⏹️ Stop button. 3. Timer displays elapsed time in HH:MM:SS format. 4. User clicks ⏹️ Stop → timer stops. Time entry is saved. 5. Time entry appears in the task's time log.
What if the user closes the browser while timer is running? Timer MUST continue running server-side. When user returns, timer displays accumulated elapsed time.
What if the timer runs for more than 8 hours? System MUST auto-stop the timer at 8 hours and display a notification: "Timer auto-stopped after 8 hours. Was this intentional?" ---

step.approach:
Phase 1 complete. State.json updated: current_phase="screens".
VH delivers user priming script: "I'm going to generate interactive mock HTML files. These use a deliberate wireframe style — dashed borders, grayscale colors, 'NOT FINAL DESIGN' banner. Focus on layout structure and behavior, not visual polish."
VH: "Let's design the Dashboard — this is the first screen users see." Interviews using screen_decomposition pattern: - Layout: sidebar + main content - Sidebar: nav links (Dashboard, Projects, Tasks, Reports, Settings) - Main: card grid of projects, time tracking widget, recent activity - Actions: Create Project (modal), Start Timer (on task cards), Search (top bar), View All Projects - Navigation targets discovered: Projects, Tasks, Reports, Settings, Create Project modal, Task Detail
VH generates screens/dashboard.md (canonical spec) and screens/dashboard.html (interactive mock). HTML uses sidebar_layout convention from CSS library. Every button has data-tooltip. Navigation links use relative paths.
For navigation targets not yet interviewed (Projects, Tasks, Reports, Settings, Task Detail), VH generates placeholder .html files immediately.
VH: "Open screens/dashboard.html in your browser." User opens it, clicks around. User: "The sidebar should be on the left — it's on the right." VH: "That's a layout structure issue — I'll fix it." Regenerates HTML with sidebar on left (swaps to correct .layout-sidebar structure). User: "The 'Create Project' button tooltip says 'Would open a modal.' What's in it?" VH: "Let's define the Create Project modal now..." Interviews, updates .md spec, regenerates HTML with modal structure and detailed tooltip. User confirms → screen locked in state.json.
VH generates placeholders for all new navigation targets. State.json planned.screens now includes: dashboard, projects, tasks, reports, settings, task-detail, create-project-modal.

VH interviews the Login screen. Generates screens/login.html. User opens it: "The blue is too bright. Can we use a softer blue? And the button corners should be more rounded. And the spacing between fields feels tight."
VH (redirecting): "Those are visual polish details — the UI Designer mode handles colors, corner radius, and exact spacing later. For now: is the email field above the password field? Is the 'Log In' button below the password field? Does the 'Forgot Password?' link point to the right screen? Does the tooltip on 'Log In' correctly describe what would happen?"
User: "Yes — the layout structure is right. The tooltips are correct." VH: "Great — Login screen locked. Moving to Projects screen."

All 14 screens designed and locked. 6 user flows documented. Edge cases covered.
Phase 5 Wire-Up: - VH regenerates all 14 screen HTML files from their .md specs - Placeholder links → real links (all screens now exist) - Cross-reference check: all features appear in screens, all entities appear in UI - Generates vision-summary.md with comprehensive index tables:
vision-summary.md Screen Index excerpt: | Screen | Purpose | Mock | Key Features | |--------|---------|------|--------------| | Dashboard | Project overview, time tracking widget, activity feed | screens/dashboard.html | Time Tracking, Project Management, Search | | Login | Email/password authentication | screens/login.html | Authentication | | Projects | Project list with create/edit/delete | screens/projects.html | Project Management | ...
User does final walkthrough: clicks through every screen in browser, confirms all tooltips and navigation. Approves complete vision.
VH reports back with: 28 features, 14 screens with interactive mocks, 7 data entities, 6 user flows, 3 flagged concerns.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Phase 0 state verification ensures deterministic behavior on every spawn.
The interactive mock IS the confirmation surface — user confirms by clicking, not by hearing text.
CSS fixation is the #1 derailment risk — redirect firmly but politely to layout/behavior.
Placeholder pages must be generated immediately to prevent broken navigation links.
Feature-inventory + per-feature detailed specs avoid the monolithic file problem.

example.name: multi_spawn_resume
example.scenario: The user completed Phase 1 (Foundation) and Phase 2 (3 screens: Dashboard, Login, Settings) in the first spawn. The context window filled up. VH saved state.json with precise last_context and used attempt_completion. The orchestrator re-spawns VH to continue.

step.approach:
VH reads vision/state.json: - current_phase: "screens" - phase_status: "in_progress" - completed.screens: dashboard (confirmed), login (confirmed), settings (in_progress) - planned.screens: [dashboard, login, settings, projects, tasks, reports, admin] - last_context: "Settings screen — discussing notification preferences section. User chose digest mode for email frequency. Next: push notification channels." - spawn_count: 1
VH cross-references: all completed files exist on disk. No discrepancies. Increments spawn_count to 2.
VH presents: "Resuming vision discovery. Phase 2 (Screens). Completed: Dashboard, Login. In progress: Settings (notification preferences). Planned: Projects, Tasks, Reports, Admin. Last topic: Settings — push notification channels. Resume from Settings screen?"
User confirms → VH continues Settings screen interview from push notifications.

VH: "We were discussing notification channels for the Settings screen. You chose digest mode for email. Now: should the app also send push notifications? If so, what events trigger them?"
Because last_context was specific ("push notification channels"), VH resumes exactly where it left off — no re-interviewing completed sections. Completes Settings screen, generates mock, user confirms → locked.

key_takeaways.takeaway: state.json + precise last_context = seamless resume across spawns.
The Phase 0 cross-reference catches disk/state drift before any work begins.
The user doesn't need to remember where they were — state.json remembers.

example.name: handling_a_stuck_user
example.scenario: The user can't decide how permissions should work. "I don't know, maybe admins can do everything and regular users can only see their own stuff?"

step.approach:
VH: "Common permission models for task management apps: 1. Role-based: Admin (full access), Member (create/edit own tasks, view all tasks in their projects), Viewer (read-only) 2. Project-level: permissions set per project — you're an admin on Project A but a viewer on Project B 3. Simple: one workspace, everyone sees everything, no restrictions Which model feels right for your app?"
User: "Role-based, but I want a fourth role: Project Manager who can manage their project but not system settings."
VH: "Let's define exactly what each role can do..." Documents in personas.md and adds role-based permissions to every feature spec and screen spec.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Never leave the user hanging with "what do you want?" — offer concrete options.
The options often spark new ideas the user hadn't considered.
